UserAccount.title: UserAccount
UserAccount.description: Represents a user account within the Snapter Games application.

id.description: Unique identifier for the user account.

Game.title: Game
Game.description: Represents a game available on the Snapter Games platform.

platform.description: The platform on which the game is available (e.g., PC, PS5, Xbox Series X).

description.description: A brief description of the game.

genre.description: The genre of the game (e.g., Action, RPG, Strategy).

Wishlist.title: Wishlist
Wishlist.description: Represents a user's wishlist of games.

gameIds.description: References to Games. (Relationship: Wishlist N:N Game)

PublishedGame.title: PublishedGame
PublishedGame.description: Represents a game published by a developer on the platform.

id.description: Unique identifier for the published game.

publisherId.description: The UID of the user who published the game.

developerName.description: The public display name of the game developer.

description.description: The full description of the game.

whatsNew.description: Notes about the latest update for the game.

downloads.description: The number of times the game has been downloaded.

averageRating.description: The average user rating for the game (0-5).

ratings.description: An array of individual rating objects.

createdAt.description: Timestamp of when the game was published.

DeveloperProfile.title: DeveloperProfile
DeveloperProfile.description: Public profile for a game developer.

id.description: The developer's user ID (matches Firebase Auth UID).

developerName.description: The public display name of the developer.

gameCount.description: The total number of games published by the developer.

definition.description: Stores user account information. Uses path-based ownership for private data. Includes email for user identification.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user, matching Firebase auth UID.

definition.description: Stores user wishlists. Path-based ownership is applied to enforce privacy. 'userId' is denormalized into the 'Wishlist' document for authorization independence.  It also includes userAccountId for quick querying, but the path `/users/{userId}/wishlists/{wishlistId}` remains the primary authorization mechanism.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user who owns the wishlist.

params.name: wishlistId
params.description: The unique identifier for the wishlist.

definition.description: Stores game information.  This collection is publicly readable but write access is controlled via Firebase Authentication and potentially DBAC (Database Authentication and Authorization via a dedicated role).

params.name: gameId
params.description: The unique identifier for the game.

definition.description: Stores games published by developers. Write access is restricted to the owning publisher, while read access can be public.

params.name: gameId
params.description: The unique identifier for the published game.

definition.description: Stores public developer profiles. Read access is public, write access is restricted to the developer.

params.name: developerId
params.description: The unique ID of the developer, matching their auth UID.

firestore.reasoning: The Firestore structure is designed to ensure authorization independence, clarity, and scalability, following the specified design principles. User accounts are stored in `/users/{userId}`, with wishlists as subcollections.  This structure avoids hierarchical authorization dependencies by relying solely on `request.auth.uid` and path-based ownership for user-specific data. Games are stored in a top-level `/games` collection. A new top-level collection `/publishedGames` is added for developer-published content, with publisher ownership enforced through security rules. A `/developers` collection is added to store public developer profiles, linking them to their games and allowing for public profile pages. Authorization Independence is achieved since there is no need for `get()` calls to check parent document data, as ownership is implied by the path for user-owned resources. The structure supports the required QAPs by segregating data based on access needs.
